findings.title: Contribution volume (12 months)
findings.evidence: 26,798 contributions in the last 12 months (12,566 commits, 2,250 pull requests) — about 73 shipped units of work every single day
findings.source: GitHub Octoverse 2023 (100M+ accounts, majority dormant per year); public country leaderboards (gayanvoice/top-github-users) where national top-1000 entry sits in the low five figures of yearly contributions
findings.analysis: 73 shipped units of work per day, every day of the year, weekends included. A typical active developer records a few hundred contributions per year; the top floors here mean this account outpaces at least 999 of every 1,000 active developers. That is not typing speed — it is an engineer who industrialized his own process.

findings.title: Integration throughput
findings.evidence: 2,250 pull requests opened in 12 months — ~43 reviewed, mergeable units of work every week
findings.source: Octoverse pull-request volume statistics; median active developer opens well under 100 PRs/year
findings.analysis: ~43 reviewed, mergeable units of work every week. Many strong professional engineers open fewer pull requests in a year than this account opens in a month.

findings.title: Repository portfolio
findings.evidence: 272 original (non-fork) repositories owned across 10 operated organizations plus the personal account — an institutional footprint
findings.source: GitHub Octoverse account totals vs. repository totals: the median account owns 0–2 public repositories; multiple public analyses of the GH Archive corpus put >30 owned repos in the top percentile of accounts
findings.analysis: The median GitHub account owns 0–2 repositories. 272 is not a large personal portfolio — it is the footprint of an engineering organization: designed, shipped, and maintained by one person.

findings.title: Language breadth
findings.evidence: 17 distinct languages each leading at least one shipped repository — led by Python (101), TypeScript (47), HTML (25), JavaScript (16), Shell (16)
findings.source: Octoverse language reports: the typical developer ships in 1–3 languages; polyglot activity beyond 5 primary languages across owned repos is rare in GH Archive analyses
findings.analysis: Most developers ship in 1–3 languages over a career. 17 languages each leading a shipped repository is the breadth of an entire team compressed into one engineer.

findings.title: Federation architecture
findings.evidence: 10 GitHub organizations operated as a single federated system — separation of concerns applied to an entire estate, not just a codebase
findings.source: GitHub organization membership statistics: most accounts belong to 0–1 organizations and *operate* none
findings.analysis: Most accounts operate zero organizations. 10 is separation-of-concerns applied to a whole estate — the org chart of a small company, run by its only employee.

findings.title: Full-stack coverage
findings.evidence: Shipped repositories lead in 5 distinct stack layers: backend, creative, frontend, infra, native — frontend through infrastructure in one pair of hands
findings.source: Layer mapping over primary languages of owned non-fork repos; covering 4+ layers with shipped code is the working definition of full-stack, and polyglot shipping at this breadth is a top-percentile behavior (see language_breadth baseline)
findings.analysis: One person covering roles a company staffs separately: frontend, backend, infrastructure, native, and creative coding. 'Full-stack' is usually a resume word; here it is a measured property.

findings.title: Python full-stack output profile — composite placement
findings.evidence: Conjunction holds: 26,798 contributions/yr (top-1% floor cleared), 272 owned repos (top-1% floor cleared), Python leads the corpus (101 repos), and shipped code spans 5 stack layers. A conjunction of independent top-1% conditions is at most as common as its rarest member — the composite top-1% claim is therefore a floor, not an estimate.
findings.source: Conjunction over the cited per-axis baselines (see METHODOLOGY.md)
findings.analysis: Read it like an underwriter: each leg alone places its holder past the 99th percentile, and holding all four simultaneously is rarer than any single leg. At most 1 engineer in 100 can make this claim; this one re-proves it by machine every morning.

findings.title: Tenure
findings.evidence: On GitHub since 2016 — 9.5 years of shipped history
findings.source: Account creation date (no percentile claimed for tenure)
findings.analysis: A decade of public, timestamped history. The output measured above is a trajectory, not a sprint — and every year of it is auditable.
